sourceQuestion:
In some programming language, an identifier is permitted to be a letter followed by any number of letters or digits. If L and D denote the sets of letters and digits respectively, which of the following expressions defines an identifier?
A. (L + D)*
B. L(L + D)*
C. LL*DD*
D. L(L + D)^+

parsed.stem: In some programming language, an identifier is permitted to be a letter followed by any number of letters or digits. If L and D denote the sets of letters and digits respectively, which of the following expressions defines an identifier?

sourceQuestion:
Which two of the following four regular expressions are equivalent? (epsilon is the empty string).
i.   (00)*(epsilon + 0)
ii.  0*
iii. 0(00)*
iv.  (00)* + (000)*

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (ii) and (iii)
C. (i) and (iii)
D. (iii) and (iv)

parsed.stem:
Which two of the following four regular expressions are equivalent? (epsilon is the empty string).
i.   (00)*(epsilon + 0)
ii.  0*
iii. 0(00)*
iv.  (00)* + (000)*

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions over {0, 1} denotes the set of all strings not containing 100 as substring?
A. 0*(1*0)*
B. 0*(10+1)*
C. 0*1*01*
D. 0*(10+1)*

parsed.stem: Which one of the following regular expressions over {0, 1} denotes the set of all strings not containing 100 as substring?

sourceQuestion:
The string 1101 does not belong to the set represented by:
A. 1(10)*(0+1)*
B. 1(0+1)*01
C. (10)*(11)*00
D. 0*(10+1)*

parsed.stem: The string 1101 does not belong to the set represented by:

sourceQuestion:
If the regular set A is represented by A = (01 + 1)* and the regular set B is represented by B = (011 + 1)*, which of the following is true?
A. A subset B
B. B subset A
C. A and B are incomparable
D. A = B

parsed.stem: If the regular set A is represented by A = (01 + 1)* and the regular set B is represented by B = (011 + 1)*, which of the following is true?

sourceQuestion:
Let S and T be languages over Sigma = {a, b} represented by the regular expressions (a + b*)* and (a + b)*, respectively. Which of the following is true?
A. T subset S
B. T = S
C. S subset T
D. S intersection T = phi

parsed.stem: Let S and T be languages over Sigma = {a, b} represented by the regular expressions (a + b*)* and (a + b)*, respectively. Which of the following is true?

sourceQuestion:
The regular expression 1*(01*)* denotes the same set as:
A. (1*0)*1*
B. 1*(10)*1
C. (1+0)*
D. None of the above

parsed.stem: The regular expression 1*(01*)* denotes the same set as:

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following languages over the alphabet {0, 1} is described by the regular expression (0+1)*00(0+1)*00(0+1)*?
A. The set of all strings containing the substring 00
B. The set of all strings containing at most two 0's
C. The set of all strings containing at least two 0's
D. The set of all strings that begin and end with 0
sourceAnswer: C. The set of all strings containing at least two 0's

parsed.stem: Which one of the following languages over the alphabet {0, 1} is described by the regular expression (0+1)*00(0+1)*00(0+1)*?

options.text: The set of all strings containing the substring 00
The set of all strings containing at most two 0's
The set of all strings containing at least two 0's
The set of all strings that begin and end with 0

sourceQuestion:
Let L = {w in {0,1}* | w is the set of all bit strings with an even number of 1s}. Which one of the regular expressions below represents L?

A. (0*10*1)*
B. 0*(10*1)*
C. 0*(10*10*)*
D. 0*(10*1)*0*

parsed.stem: Let L = {w in {0,1}* | w is the set of all bit strings with an even number of 1s}. Which one of the regular expressions below represents L?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the regular expressions given below represent the following DFA?

(DFA over {0,1}: start state q0, accepting state q2. Transitions: q0 --0--> q0, q0 --1--> q1, q1 --1--> q1, q1 --0--> q2, q2 --0--> q2, q2 --1--> q1)

I. (0+1)*1(0+1)*
II. (0+1)*10*
III. (0*1)*10*

A. I and II only
B. I and III only
C. II and III only
D. I, II and III

parsed.stem:
Which of the regular expressions given below represent the following DFA?

(DFA over {0,1}: start state q0, accepting state q2. Transitions: q0 --0--> q0, q0 --1--> q1, q1 --1--> q1, q1 --0--> q2, q2 --0--> q2, q2 --1--> q1)

I. (0+1)*1(0+1)*
II. (0+1)*10*
III. (0*1)*10*

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions represents the language: the set of all binary strings having two consecutive 0s and two consecutive 1s?

A. (0+1)*0011(0+1)* + (0+1)*1100(0+1)*
B. (0+1)*00(0+1)*11(0+1)* + (0+1)*11(0+1)*00(0+1)*
C. (0+1)*00(0+1)*11(0+1)* + (0+1)*11(0+1)*
D. (0+1)*0011(0+1)* + (0+1)*110(0+1)*

parsed.stem: Which one of the following regular expressions represents the language: the set of all binary strings having two consecutive 0s and two consecutive 1s?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions represents the set of all binary strings with an odd number of 1s?

A. 1(0(01)*0)*
B. (0+1)*1(0+1)*
C. 10*(10*10*)*
D. (0*10*10*)*0*10*

parsed.stem: Which one of the following regular expressions represents the set of all binary strings with an odd number of 1s?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following regular expressions represent(s) the set of all binary numbers that are divisible by three? Assume that the string s is divisible by three.

A. (0 + 1(01*0)*1)*
B. (0*(1(01*0)*1)*)*
C. 0*(1(01*0)*1)*
D. (1(10*1)*0)*(0 + 1(10*1)*0)*

parsed.stem: Which of the following regular expressions represent(s) the set of all binary numbers that are divisible by three? Assume that the string s is divisible by three.

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions correctly represents the language of the finite automaton given below?

[The automaton has states q1 (start, final), q2, q3, with transitions:
- q1 --a--> q2, q1 --b--> q1
- q2 --a--> q3, q2 --b--> q1
- q3 --a--> q2, q3 --b--> q3]

A. ab*a(a + ba)*
B. (b + ab*a)(a + ba)*
C. (b + ab*ab*a)*(ab*a + epsilon)
D. (b + ab*a)*(epsilon + ab*)

parsed.stem:
Which one of the following regular expressions correctly represents the language of the finite automaton given below?

[The automaton has states q1 (start, final), q2, q3, with transitions:
- q1 --a--> q2, q1 --b--> q1
- q2 --a--> q3, q2 --b--> q1
- q3 --a--> q2, q3 --b--> q3]

sourceQuestion:
Consider the Deterministic Finite-state Automaton (DFA) M shown below. The DFA runs on the alphabet {0, 1}, and has the set of states {p, q, r, s}, with p being the start state and p being the only final state.

[DFA transitions:
- p --0--> q, p --1--> s
- q --0--> p, q --1--> r
- r --0--> s, r --1--> p
- s --0--> r, s --1--> q]

Which one of the following regular expressions correctly describes the language accepted by M?

A. 10(11)^*
B. (10)^*
C. (0101)^*
D. (00 + 11 + (01 + 10)(00 + 11)^*(01 + 10))^*

parsed.stem:
Consider the Deterministic Finite-state Automaton (DFA) M shown below. The DFA runs on the alphabet {0, 1}, and has the set of states {p, q, r, s}, with p being the start state and p being the only final state.

[DFA transitions:
- p --0--> q, p --1--> s
- q --0--> p, q --1--> r
- r --0--> s, r --1--> p
- s --0--> r, s --1--> q]

Which one of the following regular expressions correctly describes the language accepted by M?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions is NOT equivalent to the regular expression (a + b + c)*?
A. (a* + b*)*c*
B. (a*b*)*c*
C. (a*b*)*(b*c*)*
D. (a + b)*(b + c)*

parsed.stem: Which one of the following regular expressions is NOT equivalent to the regular expression (a + b + c)*?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is TRUE about the regular expression 01*0?
A. It represents a finite set of finite strings.
B. It represents an infinite set of finite strings.
C. It represents a finite set of infinite strings.
D. It represents an infinite set of infinite strings.
sourceAnswer: B. It represents an infinite set of finite strings.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is TRUE about the regular expression 01*0?

options.text: It represents a finite set of finite strings.
It represents an infinite set of finite strings.
It represents a finite set of infinite strings.
It represents an infinite set of infinite strings.

sourceQuestion:
Which regular expression best describes the language accepted by the non-deterministic automaton below?
(NFA over {a, b}: start state q0, with transitions: q0 -a-> q0, q0 -b-> q0, q0 -a-> q1, q1 -b-> q2, q2 is final state)
A. (a + b)* a b
B. (ab)*a
C. b*(a + b)*
D. (a + b)*

parsed.stem:
Which regular expression best describes the language accepted by the non-deterministic automaton below?
(NFA over {a, b}: start state q0, with transitions: q0 -a-> q0, q0 -b-> q0, q0 -a-> q1, q1 -b-> q2, q2 is final state)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the regular expression R = (a + b)* (aa + bb)(a + b)*. Which one of the regular expressions given below defines the same language as defined by the regular expression R?

A. (a(ab)* + b(ba)*)*(a + b)*
B. (ab)a*
C. (a(ab)* + b(ba)*)*(b + a(ab)*(a + b))* (a + b)
D. (a(ab)* + b(ba)*)*(b + a(ab)*(b + a(ab)*(a + b)))*

parsed.stem: Consider the regular expression R = (a + b)* (aa + bb)(a + b)*. Which one of the regular expressions given below defines the same language as defined by the regular expression R?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following regular expressions describes the language over {0, 1} consisting of strings that contain exactly two 1's?

A. (0 + 1)* 1 1 (0 + 1)*
B. 0* 1 1 0*
C. 0* 1 0* 1 0*
D. (0 + 1)* 1 (0 + 1)* 1 (0 + 1)*

parsed.stem: Which of the following regular expressions describes the language over {0, 1} consisting of strings that contain exactly two 1's?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the alphabet Sigma = {0, 1}, the null/empty string lambda and the following sets of strings:
  X_0 = 1 X_1
  X_1 = 0 X_1 | 1 X_2
  X_2 = 0 X_1 | {lambda}

These are generated by the corresponding non-terminals of a regular grammar. Which of the choices below precisely represents the strings in X_0?

A. 10(0 + 10)* 1
B. 10(0 + 10)* 1 + 1
C. 1(0 + 10)* 1
D. 10(0 + 10)* 1 + 11

parsed.stem:
Consider the alphabet Sigma = {0, 1}, the null/empty string lambda and the following sets of strings:
  X_0 = 1 X_1
  X_1 = 0 X_1 | 1 X_2
  X_2 = 0 X_1 | {lambda}

These are generated by the corresponding non-terminals of a regular grammar. Which of the choices below precisely represents the strings in X_0?

sourceAnswer: C. {w | (a+b)* and n_a(w) != 0} and {eps | w = epsilon}

parsed.stem:
Consider the regular grammar below:
S -> aS | a
A -> aS | bA

The Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes for the language generated by the grammar are

options.text: {w | n_a(w) is even} and {w | n_a(w) is odd}
{w | n_a(w) is even} and {w | n_a(w) is odd} and {w | w has a b}
{w | w ends in a} and {w | w ends in b} and {epsilon}

sourceQuestion:
Let R_1 and R_2 be regular sets defined over the alphabet Sigma. Then:

A. R_1 intersection R_2 is not regular.
B. R_1 union R_2 is regular.
C. Sigma* - R_1 is regular.
D. R_1* is not regular.

parsed.stem: Let R_1 and R_2 be regular sets defined over the alphabet Sigma. Then:

options.text: R_1 intersection R_2 is not regular.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is the strongest correct statement about a finite language over some finite alphabet?

A. It could be undecidable.
B. It is Turing-machine recognizable.
C. It is a context-sensitive language.
D. It is a regular language.
E. None of the above.

parsed.stem: Which of the following is the strongest correct statement about a finite language over some finite alphabet?

sourceQuestion:
Let Sigma = {0, 1}, L = Sigma* and R = {0^1^n | n > 0}, then the languages L \ R and R are respectively:

A. regular, regular
B. not regular, regular
C. regular, not regular
D. not regular, not regular

parsed.stem: Let Sigma = {0, 1}, L = Sigma* and R = {0^1^n | n > 0}, then the languages L \ R and R are respectively:

sourceAnswer: A. L must be {a}^+ or L must be {a}^*

parsed.stem: What can be said about a regular language L over {a} whose minimal finite state automaton has two states?

options.text: L must be {a}^+ or L must be {a}^*
L must be {a^n | n is odd}
L must be {a^n | n is even}
Either L must be {a^n | n is odd} or L must be {a^n | n is even}

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two statements:

S1: {0^n | n >= 1} is a regular language
S2: {0^n 0^n 0^n | n >= 1 and n >= 1} (i.e., {0^(p+q) | p >= 1, q >= 1}) is a regular language

Which of the following statements is correct?

A. Only S1 is correct
B. Only S2 is correct
C. Both S1 and S2 are correct
D. None of S1 and S2 is correct
sourceAnswer: C. Both S1 and S2 are correct

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two statements:

S1: {0^n | n >= 1} is a regular language
S2: {0^n 0^n 0^n | n >= 1 and n >= 1} (i.e., {0^(p+q) | p >= 1, q >= 1}) is a regular language

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Both S1 and S2 are correct
None of S1 and S2 is correct

sourceAnswer: B. L = {s in {a,b}* | for every prefix s' of s, |n_a(s') - n_b(s')| <= 2}

parsed.stem: If s is a string over {a, b}* then let n_a(s) denote the number of a's in s and n_b(s) denote the number of b's in s. Which one of the following languages is NOT regular?

options.text: L = {s in {a,b}* | n_a(s) is a 3-digit prime}
L = {s in {a,b}* | for every prefix s' of s, |n_a(s') - n_b(s')| <= 2}

parsed.stem: Which of the following languages is regular?
